speaker: interviewer
text: It's August the 16th, 1995. The survivor is Mike Gluck. Masha Loen, interviewer, Los Angeles, California. [? He ?] say to be in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Masha Loen. I'm interviewing--

speaker: interviewee
text: Mike.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mike Gluck in Los Angeles, California for the Survivors of the Shoah. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: [COUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Eh, Mike, what I would like you to do-- Today is the 16th of August, 1995. I'm going to interview you, Mike Gluck. And what I want you to do is tell me your story, as a-- starting as a child and as far away as you can remember. Like, when you went to shul, or to school, or whatever-- whatever comes into your mind. But like you said, your name was Reuben and Gluck. All those things we want to know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So start as a child as far as you can remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I, uh-- as I said, I was born in 1994-- 1924 in a little town by the name Viseul. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Hold on. Well, my father was a tailor.

speaker: interviewer
text: There's some Kleenex.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the beginning, I cannot talk.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know it's hard.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: I feel that you are a survivor, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and uh-- I was-- I was brought up in a little town, very religious. I had a little sister four years younger than me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Viseul.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what country was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in the [NON-ENGLISH]. One time it was Hungary, Romania-- Hungary-- Austria-Hungary. And after the Second-- First World War, it became Romanian. And then in 1940, we were occupied by the Hungarians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, but before 1940, tell us.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Romanian. I was born there in a little town, a very religious town. And as I said, my father was a tailor. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyway-- What did your mother do, Mike?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was a housekeeper.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was a housewife.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and I went to school. And I went to elementary school. And I went to cheder. And also-- [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was the good times, too. Bad and good. So don't cry about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was good times. I mean, we were poor, but we were very happy. We were really very happy family. And then it started in the 1940. Then the Hungarian came in. I went to school. I went to cheder. And, um, there was--

speaker: interviewer
text: How was the rabbi?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was times-- there was times that I wanted to go to school. And I couldn't go because I didn't have no shoes in the wintertime. But it was OK. But we were still happy because we didn't know anything better than that. And-- and, um, also in 19-- and I-- and I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I ask you one question, Michael?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: In that little town, there were all Jews? Or there were some Poles-- I mean some Romanians, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we were a minority there.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were the minority.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were. The Jewish people were the minority in that little-- in that town. Sure, everywhere they was always more non-Jews than Jews. But we were very well-known and very-- very, uh--

speaker: interviewer
text: You kept close together?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? MG: --full ?] [? of ?] [? life. ?] Were very close. Everybody knew each other. Everybody-- in fact, we didn't have no in-- no addresses in our house because we knew exactly where people were living, and who belongs to who, what the family is. And so we went, and we were-- a happy childhood. We were-- we-- I liked baseball a lot. We used to play a lot of baseball. And when we had time to, that's the only time we had to do it is on a Saturday afternoon. And because we used to go to cheder, we used to go to school, we were always busy. And as I said, we were very happy childhood.

speaker: interviewer
text: But Saturday afternoon, didn't your father object to it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was against my father's wishes. But we were, you know, we were young people who wanted to play a little bit. So we went and played. Sometimes why-- I got an argument from my father, why I did it, why I didn't come to shul and [INAUDIBLE], and things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's all right. We all played truant.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- and then, as I-- as I remember in 1940, the Hungarians came in. And they started up the problems, a lot of problems, for the Jewish people. People were not allowed to have stores, were not allowed to go to school. And they were not allowed to do anything like human beings should-- should do. And what happened, what I want to bring out is the-- that in-- in 1944, we got the news-- not through the radio because nobody had any radios or telephones-- through the-- through someone-- loudspeaker that the Jews are not allowed to leave town, not allowed to go on trains, not allowed to leave anywhere. Just stay put where they are. And one time in 1944-- It was in--

speaker: interviewer
text: Before 1944, what did the Hungarians do? Did they let you go to stores and buy food? How did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they were-- they were-- they were rationing. I mean in 1943, it started-- started rationing the food. And the Jews were not given all the rations. We used to get sometimes from neighbors of ours, just sneaking in a little bit of flour, or-- or corn, or beans, or something like that, to be able to survive, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened before-- between '40-- You said when the Hungarians came in, it started getting bad.

speaker: interviewee
text: Bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened between '40 and '43? Did they-- did they hurt anybody?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what I said. They took all the rights away from the Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: They took

speaker: interviewee
text: They had no right to have any stores, own stores. Took everything away. And we are not allowed to go on trains. We were not allowed to go to school. Jewish people were not allowed to go to school. And, uh, we were-- we-- we didn't know what's happening. We didn't have no radios. We didn't know what's happening in the world, the news. And-- and so therefore, I mean, it's uh-- we didn't know what to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: So all of a sudden, then, we had the news that the Jews were not allowed to go nowhere. In order for us to be home, that the gendarmes-- or they call it the [HUNGARIAN] in Hungarian-- they came in. And-- and we would had the food on the stove to cook our lunch, whatever it was. And they came in just like that. They said, we arrest you. Leave everything the way it is, even the food on the stove. Nothing-- just what you have on your body. And this is how you have come-- have to come with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know what's happening. We didn't know where we gonna go, what we gonna do.

speaker: interviewer
text: But wait a moment.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father was still alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That time, my father passed away in 1942. And, uh, and I was-- I was alone. And I was with my sister. But you know, I, uh, I was visiting an uncle not far from Satu Mare that time. He took me into-- because we were alone and were orphans. And then he took, uh-- he  took me there. And from there, we went to ghetto. My aunt was still cooking the food on the stove, I remember. And the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Michael, would you repeat the-- the name of the little town where your uncle lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: That's, uh, that's, uh, that's a-- a-- a suburb from Satu Mare. It was Satu Mare.

speaker: interviewer
text: Satu Mare.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But there-- there was the [? Swabs. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: The [? Swabs. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: The [? Swabs. ?] Not Romanian, not Hungarian. The [? Swabs. ?] So anyway, I remember that-- this is what I remember exactly. We had to leave everything, leave everything the way it is. And all the people were standing outside were laughing, and having fun, and having-- and enjoying that-- what they saw. And they took us away like dogs, walking up to-- From [NON-ENGLISH], it was about 30 kilometers. They made us walk into Satu Mare, into the ghetto. And there in the ghetto was just chaos. We didn't know who it was, what we gonna do. We didn't know nothing. And all of a sudden, they made up sections. This section goes today. The other section is gonna go tomorrow. And they took us to the train station. And they put us on the train, just like cattles. 40, 50, 100 people in-- I mean, one-- one car, a carload. And we didn't know where we gonna go. They told us-- the SS came in before. They took us away that we should not worry, that gonna take us to the-- the Debrecen. It's another Hungarian city where we gonna be avoiding the persecution from the public because they are afraid that they're gonna-- they're gonna do us wrong, the [INAUDIBLE] populated from town. Instead, to go-- [? December ?] was to go-- the train should have gone this way. But we felt that the train was going this way. They locked up the doors from the cattle wagons. And they took us away like this. And the elderly people those days-- I mean, they were 50, 60, 45-year man. They said, oh my God. They didn't take us to [? December-- ?] to Debrecen. They take us somewhere else. We didn't even know. I didn't know what's going on-- was Auschwitz, as-- was [? built ?] at that time. I didn't know. So all of a sudden, we were riding in the train for about four days, I guess.

speaker: interviewer
text: You and your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister, and my family, my uncle, and with his family-- we were driving in that train for four or five days. And all of a sudden, I know before we went at that stop sign, there was changing-- [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH]-- the, uh, the, uh, the--

speaker: interviewer
text: The light-- light.

speaker: interviewee
text: The light, yeah. And then, and what happened is, they took us in. And they stopped the train. And they opened up those doors from the train, from the-- on a cattle bar wagon. And they started to scream, ausgehen, ausgehen, ausgehen! We didn't know--

speaker: interviewer
text: Get out.

speaker: interviewee
text: Leave everything in the wagon. Get out from there and don't ask any questions. The people that were there before, days before, they would have the stripes on uniform. We didn't know what the striped uniform was either. And we saw those people. So the SS people, they're starting to scream at us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me, Mike. I had to interrupt.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's track back to get the ghetto that you were in.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: In that ghetto, did they have just one little town put in that ghetto? Or were the collection of other Jews--

speaker: interviewee
text: Collection from all around Satu Mare. The Jewish people, they were all around-- Satu Mare, the whole suburbs from Satu Mare. All the people, they got together and they put in one camp, in one ghetto in Satu Mare. You see?

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry for having to interrupt. Now we're at Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and then we are-- and then we-- what happened is that we went out, and we didn't know what happened. We didn't know what happened. So and, Ausgehen! And to-- the people who can work, they should stay in one line. And the people with kids and Frauen, and, uh, women, and kids, and older people, they should stay in another line.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we stood. And I stood with my uncle in one line. Well, they made five people in a row. And I was standing there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'll for-- don't forget that-- I'll never forget this in my life. He had a little daughter. She was 12 years old. Redhead with braids, braids in the back. She was a beautiful little girl. And she-- when she walked by-- and she walked by with her mom, and they had another little boy by the name Yankele-- went by. [CRYING] When she walked by, I grabbed her. And I kissed little [NON-ENGLISH]. And I felt--

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean they were sending her to the other side?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was walking by me, like this-- here by us there. The women and the older people, they was by-- walking by. And I says-- her name was [NON-ENGLISH]. Leah. I grabbed and I kissed her. And I didn't anything. But I felt, I'll never see her again. I never see her again.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they walked by. We didn't know. But we heard from far away a smell. And we hear from far away music and screaming. So there was another SS. I didn't know what it was. A German or a [? Swab ?] or somebody there on the side and were watching us. And they had the nerve while we were standing in line to wait to get into the, uh, to the, uh, undressing room there, whatever. And I asked the guy, the SS man, I says, in German, I says, what's going on? What's going on up there? Why the screaming, or why the music? And why that smell? Just looked at me, and smiled, and didn't say anything. We walked a little by there, a little further. And I saw a lady there-- there on the, uh, edge of the street there, holding a little doll in her hand and crying. Where's my baby? Where's my baby? So I just thought to myself, how this possible? I mean, what happened here? What's going to happen here? So they took us in into a big, big hall. And they told-- they told us in-- told us to undress, to bare undress, completely. And I still remember that.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans, to show us what our situation is, they brought in a poor man from the outside with an SS. Well, the guy brought him in. And they bent him over a chair. And the SS with those boots, and with those things, you know, with those handkerchiefs and the gloves they got, got a stick in his hand. And he gave him 25 in front of all of us. That man was screaming till he could scream--

speaker: interviewer
text: We were naked--

speaker: interviewee
text: --until he could not scream.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm so sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were naked. We were naked, and look-- to look-- to watch what he does. And then he walked away like he is God. He-- he-- he owns the world, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I asked my uncle, what's going to happen? I says-- he says, I don't know what's gonna happen here. I don't know what's gonna happen. Then they shaved us all over. And then they put some-- some things. I don't-- They told us for the lice. The lice. And right away, after 15 minutes, we didn't recognize each other. Why? It's because we were differently dressed. The same outfit, the same cap, the same-- the-- uh, striped uniform. And I looked out. I says, where's my uncle? What's this? I didn't recognize this. I'm here. We did not recognize each other. So they took us into a barrack. And they gave us the orders. And the law is this. When you walk on the street, and then you-- you see a German officer walking by, you have to take off your cap, bend your head, and wait till he passes by. You know. So this order, and that was-- we should-- we're gonna have an Appell every morning, 3 o'clock in the morning. We have to get up. And they took us into that barracks. And there were people from before, a few days there before. Just like animals. Just like-- an-- even worse than animals. And I says to my uncle, what is this? What's going on? He says, I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they serve us some food somehow. I don't know what kind of food it was. And I was still-- you know, my feelings still at home with my better life. And I couldn't eat that food. I could not. I thought maybe there is a mouse in there, or a-- a rat, or something like that. That's what I felt. And I couldn't eat. And my uncle started sc-- screaming, eat it! You've got to have something in your stomach. I says, I can't eat it. So they guys that were there before a few days, they says, let him have-- let him-- let him be. After two or three days, he'll eat it. He will eat it. And so it happened. After three or four days, I was glad they gave me anything. And after five days being in Auschwitz, so-called Birkenau, they give an order there are 1,000 Hungarian men, Jewish men, have to stand in Appell. And we stood in Appell-- 1,000 Jewish men-- and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they say Hungarians?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hungarian, they said. So strictly Hungarian Jews have to stay in line. And 1,000. They counted us, 1,000. And they says, aufgehen!

speaker: interviewer
text: That was--

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went to the train station. And they put us in the cattle wagon, but no more than 50 men in a wagon. No more. And they took us to Plaszow. After being five days in this area--

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: They put you in that room. And they undressed you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they shower you first before they--

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't shower me at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: They just put on that-- that material, that slick-- that liquid stuff there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Powder?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They didn't-- they didn't shower us. Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nothing-- They just--

speaker: interviewee
text: Just put us on the-- the-- the-- our uniform, and they make us go into the-- the barracks, whatever there was. I don't remember--

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: --which one it was. So there-- from there, they took us to Plaszow. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: When?

speaker: interviewer
text: To Plaszow.

speaker: interviewee
text: About two hours. About an hour and 1/2 or two hours. That's all. That's all it took. And they told us to get out. And we went on the-- on the-- on the Appellplatz. And there was people with tables asking us our name and what's my occupation. If you didn't have an occupation, you went to the Arbeitskraft, Arbeitslager, whatever it was. But if you had an-- an-- an occupation, they took you into-- to-- to do your occupation job. And my uncle was a shoemaker. And I didn't know. And he says, what's your [INAUDIBLE]. And my-- my uncle says, he's a shoemaker. So they put me in in the factory for the shoes. I didn't know how to make shoes. I didn't know how to do that. But they had a few inmates, the Jewish people, who knew that I don't know how to make shoes. And they were there for a longer time. And every one of them showed me how to do it, just to make the shoe to show that I'm doing something in front of the SS.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your uncle was with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: My uncle was always with me there. And he showed me how to do it and clop it on the [INAUDIBLE] and clop it in.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we stayed there for about three months. They fed us pretty good there in the Plaszow. Yeah. And, uh, I remember that-- that our commandant by the name [? Goeth, ?] [? Goeth, ?] whatever the name is, he should have never been warden. That animal. I remember when we came home from work, he was staying on his balcony with no shirt on. Just drunk, smoking cigarette, and aiming whoever he could catch. The next man to me-- the next-- the man next to me [SNAPS] got it, go, fell down. And I said, oh my God. What is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was laughing. He was drinking. And he was standing on the balcony. He just kept on shooting the way we were marching back from work, you know. That time, I found out his name. His name is [? Goeth, ?] you know. What have-- and so we stayed there. We were there for about three months. Then the Russians come closer and closer, come closer. Then there was an order that we're not allowed to leave the barracks. If we have to go to the bathroom in the middle of the night, only naked. If you're naked, then they wouldn't shoot you. If you have a little clothes, they were thinking that the partisans coming in or something. So they didn't want-- you know. So they told us to go to the bathroom naked. So this was the sign that we are inmates from the camp, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: From Plaszow.

speaker: interviewee
text: From Plaszow. And, um, we stood there. We stayed there for about three months. And then they got together, got us together in the Appellplatz. And they told us we're gonna leave this camp. So we stood in Appell for about three, four hours in middle of the day. Or I think till the evening, till it got dark. And then I remember to go, where we stood there in line, to get up to the train was a little hill-- a little-- a little hill to go up there. And it was, you know, not a straight-- a straight running. It was dark. We didn't see. And both sides, there were Germans, SS, with the sticks. And beating us, no matter where. People were felling down. We were running. And we didn't know what to do. We didn't know where to run. We didn't know where we're gonna run. In the meantime, they beat us with a stick. Lucky me, I-- I-- I hid myself. And I didn't get hit, not once even. I fell down, and I got up, and I run right away. We got into the, uh, to the-- to the-- to the cattle wagon, there like you call it. And around 8 o'clock in the morning, they served us a-- a conserve with meat-- with meat-- and a piece of bread. No water. Very salty. The meat was salty like hell. And they kept us in the wagon in the sun, under the sun, a whole day, and not given us a drop of water. Everybody was going crazy. We didn't know what to do. We didn't have no water.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was the train station near Plaszow.

speaker: interviewee
text: They take us from Plaszow to Mauthausen. We didn't know we gonna go to Mauthausen. But we were on the train--

speaker: interviewer
text: But that was on the train--

words.text: But that was on the train--

speaker: interviewee
text: --to go somewhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: Somewhere.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And when-- at night, as the train started to go, and I remember even then, I was so thirsty. I didn't know what-- I'm going out my mind. And I have a little bot-- a little cup or something. And I urinate in the cup. And I thought, maybe I'm gonna drink a little-- you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's nothing new. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I drank it, and I threw up. I couldn't take it. By the morning, we stopped somewhere in the station. They opened up the wagon, the doors. And the SS was waiting for us-- some SS. They came up with the beat-- beat with the sticks. And beat us. And they accused us, what we did something. And one man, a Jewish man, I remember, he says, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. He started to scream.

speaker: interviewer
text: He said, I didn't do nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: He just-- he accused him of something. He says, I'm here. [INAUDIBLE] do. [? I got my name. ?] What? I didn't do anything. You have a nerve! What-- you-- he started beat him, beat him, until he beat him to death in front of us. In front of us. And then we-- we knew already about what's going on. And then the SS were waiting outside. And some SS-- I do remember. When they gave us a little water to drink, each one a little drink. Some SS-- I realize, [? now ?] I looked at their faces, I think it was that impression that they felt sorry for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Some of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of them, yes. Some of them, they did felt like sorry for us. Why? So anyway, so we left that station. And we headed up to Mauthausen. We got off the train. Of course the SS were waiting for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they tell you that you're going to Mauthausen?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know. We-- we didn't know what. And we saw when we got into the camp, it says Mauthausen Arbeit macht frei, was written up there.

speaker: interviewer
text: 'Work makes the life free.'

speaker: interviewee
text: Free. And the SS, aufgehen! Aufgehen! Aufgehen! We were not supposed to look on the side. We were not supposed to see anything. Just from the station. From the station, go into the car-- into the camp car, the Mauthausen. And we were scattered around the streets around the camp. And I remember that, too. It was evening. And then-- and one SS man came with a German Shepherd. I remember that, too. Quite frankly, I remember that. And he started to say to that dog, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. And a man there, a Jewish man, I don't know who the man is. And he started to grab here. The man tried to pull away himself. He couldn't. The more he pulled, the more he ripped his flesh out.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean he told the dog to grab that man?

speaker: interviewee
text: He called the dog that-- he is the mench, and the mench is the dog.

speaker: interviewer
text: The dog.

speaker: interviewee
text: You see what I mean? So that's what I remember that, too. And I was standing there, and I'm looking at myself. And I look at him. And I says, [? that, ?] why? Look at this here. And the SS stands there. He must have a good dinner or something to [? get ?] that fun. So [INAUDIBLE] ripped him up to pieces. And he left him there. And he walked away. That was his satisfaction.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean that man has never done anything to that SS man. Why? How could this happen? I mean, how could people become such a brutal nation? I mean, such sadistic feelings towards people had never done anything wrong to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, anyway, we stayed there in Mauthausen-- I don't know-- I think it's about five, six days. It came the next morning. It-- well-- next morning, we got up. First of all, we had to sleep like this. We had to stay in the line outside. And we had only our underwear-- no clothes on. Only our underwear.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they take your clothes away from you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And then we stood there in the line. And that was the room over there in the wall. There was one man--

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me, Mike. Would you tell me how, when you came down the train, did they undress you? What did they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they took us in a [? block. ?] There they tell us-- told us to take off our clothes. Only the underwear, we should go around on our underwear. We should not use no clothes. And then to go to sleep, we had to wait in a line outside. And there was the wardroom. And there was the wall. And when we got there to the door, I saw what's going on. One man was laying with his head this way. And one man had to lay with his head this way with his feet, this way. But like sardines. Not enough flat-- only sideways. And if you didn't push up yourself towards the other man next to you, there was an SS who give you a smack. Up, much closer.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it? Like cots?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing! On the floor!

speaker: interviewer
text: On the floor.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the floor!

speaker: interviewer
text: On the floor.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the floor! When-- and I saw that, and I says, OK, he's laying like this. Here, I have to lay this way. So I run there. And I push myself up like this here. Like this, I push myself up there. So he didn't hit me. He didn't hit me because I did my job.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean to tell me that all the people were squeezed in together--

speaker: interviewee
text: Like this, the whole room!

speaker: interviewer
text: In the barrack? What was it?

words.text: In the barrack? What was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like this, like this, like this, like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it? Like a barrack?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a barrack, yeah, in a room.

speaker: interviewer
text: In a room?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And we couldn't sleep. So apparently, we scattered around somehow. And a lot of people died for suffocation. So we have enough room to sleep there, to sleep the so-called sleep on the floor. In the morning, we got up. We got a little slice--

speaker: interviewer
text: A lot of people died there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Died! A lot of people died in that room because-- from suffocation, you know. It-- it's probably never told to anybody, but I was there. I saw that. I saw what they did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mike, was that the hundred men-- the 1,000 men that got--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's from the-- that's from the-- no, no. That's from whole Plaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: From whole Plaszow.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was Hungarian and Polish all together. And-- and so in the morning, we got up. We were told to stand in Appell. And then they give us some-- a little coffee, whatever they call it. Black water and a little slice of bread. And they took us in. They had us march into the stone-- [? stonegrum. ?] They call it [? stonegrum. ?].
